Puzzle #2 Civil Disobedience
📢

Picket the White House

Use civil disobedience to gain attention for the suffrage movement

"Governments derive their just powers from the consent of the governed."
Banner carried by suffragette picketers — Independence Day, 1917

Suffragists picket the White House, 1917 · Harris & Ewing · Library of Congress

Women had been regularly picketing the White House since 1913 — but once the United States entered World War I, everything changed.

In 1917, the police began arresting women outside the White House. Undaunted by these arrests, women marched to the White House on Independence Day, carrying banners reading "Governments derive their just powers from the consent of the governed" — and were promptly arrested.

Arrests at the gates of the White House did nothing to stop the daily gathering of women picketers. More women were arrested and given sentences varying from six days to six months. Most of those arrested were sent to Occoquan Workhouse in Virginia.

On arrival at the workhouse, women refused to put on prison uniforms or work. The guards became violent, kicking and beating prisoners in what became known in the suffrage movement as "The Night of Terror."

Women resorted to a hunger strike. After refusing food, they were force-fed — some had a tube forced through the nose rather than through the mouth, resulting in bleeding and injury. Upon their release, many were too weak to walk on their own.

Other tactics to suppress outspoken women included labeling them as mentally unstable and threatening to send them to institutions for the insane.

Source: Library of Congress

Timeline of Resistance

1913

Women begin regularly picketing the White House, demanding the right to vote.

1917 — April

U.S. enters World War I. Police begin arresting White House picketers.

1917 — July 4th

Women march on Independence Day with constitutional banners. Mass arrests follow.

1917 — Nov.

The Night of Terror at Occoquan Workhouse. Guards beat imprisoned suffragettes.

1918

Arrests ruled unconstitutional. Hunger strikers released. Public opinion shifts.
